mnemonics.title: P vs NP Meaning
mnemonics.content: P = Quickly Solve, NP = Quickly Check. Like finding vs. verifying a word in a crossword puzzle.

mnemonics.title: NP-Completeness Recipe
mnemonics.content: To prove NP-Complete: First Verify (in NP), then Reduce (a known NP-complete to your problem). Remember: VR - Verify then Reduce!

mnemonics.title: Polynomial Properties
mnemonics.content: Polynomials are Nice: Closed under Addition, Multiplication, and Composition. Keep this in mind when combining algorithms.

acronyms.usage: Class of problems solvable efficiently - O(n^k)
Class of problems where solutions are verifiable efficiently
First NP-complete problem - given boolean formula, can it be true?
Formula as AND of ORs - (A OR B) AND (C OR D)
Find shortest tour visiting all cities - classic NP-hard problem
Find if graph has clique of size k - NP-complete
Find minimum vertices touching all edges - NP-complete

visualTips.visualization: Draw concentric circles: P inside NP. Unknown if they're equal - draw a question mark at the boundary.
Think of NP-complete problems as 'most difficult' in NP. Show a chain: SAT → 3-SAT → CLIQUE → VERTEX-COVER, each reducing to the next.
Graph n (x-axis) vs time (y-axis): linear n, quadratic n² (curves up slowly), exponential 2^n (shoots up dramatically). Show where 'practical' ends.
Picture a machine that takes problem A as input, transforms it to problem B format, then runs B's solver - all in poly-time.

analogies.analogy: Like finding a word in a sorted dictionary - can be done efficiently through systematic search.
Like verifying someone else's solution to a puzzle - much easier than finding it yourself.
Like being the 'master key' - if you can solve one, you can solve all NP problems.
Like using a universal translator - convert any problem into another language (problem) that a solver can understand.
Trying every possible key to open a lock - works for few keys but impossible for many.
